behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at module handoff (file discovery -> sort -> execution), not just one pure function. REAL-INFRA: no real network/OS primitive/subprocess behavior is required beyond local filesystem+DB wiring. MOCK-HIDE: a unit test of sort alone can miss wrong execution order after discovery/filtering. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no, (b) no, (c) no, (d) no. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text only requires ordering by filename prefix, not real subprocess/network effects. Adversarial critique: if implementation exposes a single pure sorter used directly by runner, Unit could be enough.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner against a temp migrations directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., 001, 010, 002) and migrations that append their id into a DB audit table; assert applied order is 001->002->010 in persisted execution results.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior depends on interaction between migration executor and _migrations metadata persistence across runs. REAL-INFRA: can be caught with local DB/filesystem wiring; no OS/network-only failure required. MOCK-HIDE: mocking metadata repo may hide mismatched read/write contract. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no, (b) no, (c) no, (d) no. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is about idempotent re-run logic, not subprocess/real network conditions. Adversarial critique: if this is only a deterministic predicate on an in-memory applied-set, Unit could suffice.
behaviors.test_description: Apply migrations once, capture side effects, run runner again on same DB, assert no migration SQL executes again and _migrations row count does not increase.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure appears at transaction manager + migration execution + runner loop boundary. REAL-INFRA: requires DB transaction behavior, but still local DB/module wiring; no external network/OS behavior required by text. MOCK-HIDE: fake transaction layers often report rollback without proving DB state actually reverted. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no explicit OS/network/subprocess requirement, (b) no, (c) no, (d) no. REJECTION-GATE: considered System; rejected because behavior text only requires DB rollback/stop semantics within runner, not real subprocess or distributed conditions. Adversarial critique: if migrations use Postgres-specific DDL transactional edge cases, only a real Postgres process may expose failures, pushing to System.
behaviors.test_description: Use three migrations where #2 intentionally fails after a prior write in same migration; run once and assert: writes from #2 are absent (rolled back), #3 was never applied, and _migrations records only #1.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is a concurrency race across independent processes plus shared DB state. REAL-INFRA: yes; needs real process-level parallelism/locking timing. MOCK-HIDE: in-process mocks or single-thread simulation can miss true interleavings and lock behavior. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) yes -> System, (b) no, (c) no, (d) no. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly says 'two instances ... at the same time', which requires process-level concurrency semantics. Adversarial critique: if runner exposes lock acquisition API and concurrency is deterministically modeled in one process, Integration might catch some races, but not the real boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes concurrently against the same test Postgres and same migrations directory; assert every migration side effect appears once and _migrations has one row per migration with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is bootstrapping order across schema-initialization and migration-application modules. REAL-INFRA: local DB/filesystem wiring is sufficient; no OS/network/subprocess-specific failure required by behavior text. MOCK-HIDE: unit tests of each module can pass while startup sequencing is wrong. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no, (b) no, (c) no, (d) no. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because text scopes to first-run initialization ordering, not external infrastructure behavior. Adversarial critique: if creation is a single SQL string emitted by one function, Unit might be sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Against an empty DB, run with one migration that expects _migrations to exist (or inspect command order via DB state) and assert _migrations exists before first migration record/write occurs.
